1: Standard Sinaitic address formula — YHWH speaks to Moses alone for the post-birth purification law. The transition from the dietary laws (addressed to Aaron and Moses jointly) to the birth-purity laws (Moses alone) reflects the scope: the dietary laws required priestly enforcement; the birth-purity law primarily affects the mother and is administered by the priest only at the offering stage (Leviticus 12:6).
2: isha ki tazria veyaldah zakhar: 'When a woman bears seed and gives birth to a male.' The verb tazria (third-person feminine hiphil of zara', to sow/plant seed) is literally 'when she sows seed' or 'when she causes seed to sprout' — a striking agricultural metaphor applied to human conception. The chapter heading (ki tazria) is the Hebrew name for this Torah portion. The comparison keniddat devatah (as during her menstruation) indicates a seven-day impurity period — the birth itself generates the same impurity category as menstruation (niddah).
3: uvayom hashemini yimmol besar orlatah: 'On the eighth day the flesh of his foreskin shall be circumcised.' The eighth-day circumcision rule connects the birth-purity law to the Abrahamic covenant (Genesis 17:12). The timing is significant: the mother's seven-day impurity period ends, and on the eighth day — the first day of her 33-day blood-purification period — the male child enters the covenant. The eighth day is theologically loaded in Leviticus and later tradition as a day of new beginning (Leviticus 9:1 — Aaron's inaugural offering; Leviticus 14:10 — leper's purification completion).
4: shloshet ushloshem yom teshev bidemei toharah: 'thirty-three days she shall remain in the blood of her purification.' Demei toharah (blood of purification) is a nuanced phrase — the blood is called 'blood of purification' even though it is still associated with ritual impurity. The mother is in an intermediate state: no longer in the severe impurity of niddah but not yet fully restored to holiness. She may not touch holy things or enter the sanctuary — the two primary locations of YHWH's presence. The total impurity period for a son: 7 + 33 = 40 days.
5: ve'im-neqevah teled vetame'a shnayim kishnuatah: 'if she gives birth to a girl, she shall be unclean two weeks, as in her menstruation.' The impurity period doubles for a daughter — 14 days of niddah-equivalent impurity followed by 66 days of blood-purification, totaling 80 days. The reason for the doubled period is not explained in the text and has generated extensive rabbinic discussion. It may reflect the understanding that a female child carries her own future reproductive capacity, or it may reflect the ritual weight of the female body's future role. The asymmetry is acknowledged but not rationalized in the text itself.
6: uvimlo'ot yemei toharatah levin o levat: 'when the days of her purification are complete, for a son or a daughter.' The purification offering consists of two animals: a year-old lamb (keves ben-shenato) for a burnt offering (olah) and a young dove or turtledove (ben-yonah or tor) for a sin offering (ḥatta't). The lamb is the standard offering of someone fully reintegrating into the covenant community; the accompanying sin offering addresses not moral sin but the ritual impurity state of childbirth. The two-offering structure (restoration + purity-cleansing) mirrors other purification rituals in Leviticus.
7: vekhipper aleiha hakohen vetahera: 'the priest shall make atonement for her and she will be clean.' Kipper (atonement) is the central verb of Levitical sacrifice — it covers, removes, or ransoms guilt and impurity. Here it applies to birth-impurity rather than moral sin, confirming that kipper operates across both sin and ritual-impurity categories. zot torat hayoledet: 'this is the law for the one who gives birth' — the summary formula (torah) paralleling v. Leviticus 11:46. The law applies universally: every birth, regardless of sex, requires this purification ritual.
8: ve'im-lo timsah yadah dei she: 'if she cannot afford a lamb.' The poverty provision offers two turtledoves (tor) or two young pigeons (bnei yonah) as a substitution — one for the burnt offering and one for the sin offering. This provision is directly invoked in Luke 2:24: when Mary and Joseph bring Jesus to the temple for Mary's purification and Jesus's presentation, they offer 'a pair of turtledoves, or two young pigeons' (Luke 2:24, citing Leviticus 12:8) — signaling that Joseph and Mary were poor. The one who would become the ultimate offering was presented in the temple under the provision for the poor.
